content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 47 (GEN_047)

## 1. Miqtseh Echav — "From the End of" vs. "From Among" vs. "From the Full Complement of" (v. 2)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from the end of his brothers" | Literal: miqtseh = "from the end/extremity" |
| LOGOS | "from among his brothers" | Neutral idiomatic rendering |
| PNEUMA | "from the full complement of his brothers" | Interpretive — one possible reading |
| **SOPHIA** | **"from among his brothers" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew miqtseh echav is genuinely ambiguous: it may mean "from the extremity" (i.e., the least impressive), "from some of," or "from the total group." EMET's "from the end" is too literal to function clearly in English without a gloss — an English reader would not understand what "from the end of his brothers" means. PNEUMA's "from the full complement" resolves the ambiguity toward one specific interpretation. LOGOS's "from among" is the most neutral rendering: it conveys selection without forcing an interpretive decision. |

## 2. Kesef — "Silver" vs. "Money" (vv. 14, 15, 16, 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "silver" throughout | kesef = silver — the physical metal |
| LOGOS | "money" throughout | kesef as medium of exchange |
| PNEUMA | "money" throughout | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"money" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | Hebrew kesef means both "silver" (the metal) and "money" (the medium of exchange). In the famine-economy narrative (vv. 13-26), kesef functions as the medium of exchange — it is spent, exhausted, and gone. The narrative logic tracks the sequential loss of economic resources: first money, then livestock, then land. "Money" serves this narrative arc more clearly than "silver," which in modern English suggests the material rather than the function. The Hebrew reader would have understood kesef as "money" in this commercial context. |

## 3. Lefi Ha-Taf — "According to the Mouth of the Little Ones" vs. "According to Their Dependents" (v. 12)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to the mouth of the little ones" | Literal: lefi = "to the mouth of," taf = "little ones" |
| LOGOS | "according to the number of their dependents" | Interprets as a counting formula |
| PNEUMA | "according to the number of their dependents" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"according to the number of their dependents" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The phrase lefi ha-taf is an idiom meaning "proportional to household size." While EMET's literal rendering preserves the Hebrew image, "according to the mouth of the little ones" is opaque in English — the reader cannot tell whether it refers to the quantity of food, the type of food, or the number of people. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "according to the number of their dependents" captures the established meaning. The word taf ("little ones") is a synecdoche for the whole dependent household; "dependents" preserves this. |

## 4. Adamah — "Soil" vs. "Land" (vv. 18, 19, 20, 22, 23, 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "soil" for adamah throughout; "land" for erets | Distinguishes the two Hebrew nouns |
| LOGOS | "land" for both adamah and erets | Standard English; no distinction |
| PNEUMA | "land" for both | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"soil" for adamah — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew deliberately uses two different nouns in the famine-economy section: erets ("land, country, territory") and adamah ("soil, cultivatable ground"). The Egyptians sell their adamah (arable soil, cultivated ground) to Pharaoh — not their erets (the nation/territory of Egypt). The distinction is theologically loaded: adamah is the word from which Adam is formed (Gen 2:7); it is the earth that bears crops and sustains life. Conflating adamah with erets in English loses the Hebrew's precision. SOPHIA follows EMET: "soil" for adamah, "land" for erets. |

## 5. Hissigu — "Reached" vs. "Attained to" vs. "Matched" (v. 9)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "have not reached" | hiphil of nasag = to reach, overtake |
| LOGOS | "have not attained to" | Same root; formal English register |
| PNEUMA | "have not matched" | Interpretive — comparison rather than reaching |
| **SOPHIA** | **"have not attained to" — LOGOS upheld** | The hiphil of nasag means "to reach, to overtake, to attain" — it implies a goal that was not met, a mark that was not reached. "Reached" (EMET) is accurate but slightly flat. "Attained to" (LOGOS) preserves the aspiration-and-falling-short nuance in natural formal English. "Matched" (PNEUMA) shifts the metaphor from reaching toward a target to comparison between equals — a different image from the Hebrew. |

## 6. Veshakhavti — "I Will Lie Down" vs. "Let Me Lie" vs. "When I Lie Down" (v. 30)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But I will lie down with my fathers" | weqatal as future declarative |
| LOGOS | "But let me lie with my fathers" | Renders as volitional/cohortative |
| PNEUMA | "When I lie down with my fathers" | Renders as temporal clause |
| **SOPHIA** | **"When I lie down with my fathers" — PNEUMA upheld** | The Hebrew veshakhavti is a weqatal (converted perfect) following the negative imperative al-na tiqbereni ("do not bury me in Egypt"). In this syntactic sequence, the weqatal functions as a continuation of the instruction: "Do not bury me in Egypt — [but] when I lie down with my fathers, carry me out." PNEUMA's temporal rendering best captures the logical flow: Jacob is describing the sequence of events he wants Joseph to follow. EMET's bare future declarative ("I will lie down") is grammatically possible but disconnects from the imperative chain. LOGOS's "let me lie" introduces a volitional mood not present in the form. |

## 7. Vayishtachu — "Bowed Down" vs. "Bowed Himself" vs. "Bowed in Worship" (v. 31)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and Israel bowed down" | Simple rendering of hishtaphel |
| LOGOS | "Then Israel bowed himself" | Reflexive preserved |
| PNEUMA | "And Israel bowed in worship" | Interpretive — resolves the worship/obeisance ambiguity |
| **SOPHIA** | **"and Israel bowed himself" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hishtaphel of shachah means "to bow oneself down" — a reflexive form that can denote worship toward God or obeisance toward a person. The context is deliberately ambiguous: Jacob may be worshiping God in gratitude for Joseph's oath, or bowing toward Joseph, or both. EMET's "bowed down" is adequate but loses the reflexive. PNEUMA's "bowed in worship" resolves the ambiguity in one direction — the Hebrew leaves it open. LOGOS's "bowed himself" preserves both the reflexive form and the ambiguity, letting the reader hold both possibilities. |

---

*Genesis 47: 7 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with LOGOS alone on 4 items (v. 2 from-among, v. 9 attained-to, v. 31 bowed-himself, v. 14 money), with EMET alone on 1 item (adamah/soil), with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 1 item (v. 12 dependents), and with PNEUMA alone on 1 item (v. 30 when-I-lie-down). Full agreements (3-0) include: "few and evil" (v. 9), "sojournings" for megurai (v. 9), "covenant-faithfulness and truth" for chesed ve-emet (v. 29), "put your hand under my thigh" (v. 29), "at the head of the bed" for al-rosh ha-mittah (v. 31 — all three follow MT against LXX), "he moved them to the cities" (v. 21 — all follow MT against LXX), "were fruitful and multiplied greatly" (v. 27), and the double blessing of Pharaoh (vv. 7, 10).*
